attachments.name: Create design system
attachments.content:
We will create a design system in this project.

**Company description:** Tan Lerida: An experienced lifestyle leather brand catering to the premium mass market. Launching in australian market.

**Uploaded files** (read via the project filesystem):
- `uploads/Untitled design (2).jpg`
- `uploads/Untitled design (1).jpg`
- `uploads/Untitled design.jpg`
- `uploads/TL Emblem (1).png`
- `uploads/Final Logo (2).png`
- `uploads/brand (1).md`

---

Design systems are folders on the file system containing typography guidelines, colors, assets, brand style and tone guides, css styles, and React recreations of UIs, decks, etc. they give design agents the ability to create designs against a company's existing products, and create assets using that company's brand. Design systems should contain real visual assets (logos, brand illustrations, etc), low-level visual foundations (e.g. typography specifics; color system, shadow, border, spacing systems) and also high-level visual ELEMENTS (buttons, full screens) within ui kits.

No need to invoke the create_design_system skill; this is it.

To begin, create a todo list with the tasks below, then follow it:

- Explore provided assets and materials to gain a high-level understanding of the company/product context, the different products represented, etc. Read each asset (codebase, figma, file etc) and see what they do. Find some product copy; examine core screens; find any design system definitions.
- Create a README.md with the high-level understanding of the company/product context, the different products represented, etc. Mention the sources you were given: full Figma links, codebase paths, etc. Do not assume the reader has access, but store in case they do.
- Call set_project_title with a short name derived from the brand/product (e.g. "Acme Design System"). This replaces the generic placeholder so the project is findable.
- IF any slide decks attached, use your repl tool to look at them, extract key assets + text, write to disk.
- Explore the codebase and/or figma design contexts and create a colors_and_type.css file containing CSS vars for both base type + color styles (e.g. fg1, fg2, serif-display, etc) and semantic CSS vars (e.g. h1, h2, code, p). Copy any webfonts or ttfs into fonts/.
- Explore, then update README.md with a CONTENT FUNDAMENTALS section: how is copy written? What is tone, casing, etc? I vs you, etc? are emoji used? What is the vibe? Include specific examples
- Explore, update README.md with VISUAL FOUNDATIONS section that talks about the visual motifs and foundations of the brand. Colors, type, spacing, backgrounds (images? full-bleed? hand-drawn illustrations? repeating patterns/textures? gradients?), animation (easing? fades? bounces? no anims?), hover states (opacity, darker colors, lighter colors?), press states (color? shrink?), borders, inner/outer shadow systems, protection gradients vs capsules, layout rules (fixed elements), use of transparency and blur (when?), color vibe of imagery (warm? cool? b&w? grain?), corner radii, what do cards look like (shadow, rounding, border), etc. whatever else you can think of. answer ALL these questions.
- If you are missing font files, find the nearest match on Google Fonts. Flag this substitution to the user and ask for updated font files.
- As you work, create HTML card files in preview/ that populate the Design System tab. Target ~700×150px each (400px max) — err toward MORE small cards, not fewer dense ones. Split at the sub-concept level: separate cards for primary vs neutral vs semantic colors; display vs body vs mono type; spacing tokens vs a spacing-in-use example; one card per component state cluster. A typical system is 12–20+ cards. Skip titles and framing — the asset name renders OUTSIDE the card, so just show the swatches/specimens/tokens directly with minimal decoration. After writing each batch, call register_assets with items carrying viewport {width: 700, height: <your estimate>}, a one-line subtitle, and a `group` tag so the Design System tab can split cards into sections. Use these groups: "Type" for typography specimens and scales, "Colors" for palettes / color scales / semantic colors, "Spacing" for radii / shadow systems / spacing tokens / elevation, "Components" for buttons / form inputs / cards / badges / menus, "Brand" for logos / imagery / anything that doesn't fit the others. Title-cased, consistent across the batch.
- Copy logos, icons and other visual assets into assets/. update README.md with an ICONOGRAPHY describing the brand's approach to iconography. Answer ALL these and more: are certain icon systems used? is there a builtin icon font? are there SVGs used commonly, or png icons? (if so, copy them in!) Is emoji ever used? Are unicode chars used as icons? Make sure to copy key logos, background images, maybe 1-2 full-bleed generic images, and ALL generic illustrations you find. NEVER draw your own SVGs or generate images; COPY icons programmatically if you can.
- For icons: FIRST copy the codebase's own icon font/sprite/SVGs into assets/ if you can. Otherwise, if the set is CDN-available (e.g. Lucide, Heroicons), link it from CDN. If neither, substitute the closest CDN match (same stroke weight / fill style) and FLAG the substitution. Document usage in ICONOGRAPHY.
- For each product given (E.g. app and website), create UI kits in ui_kits/<product>/{README.md, index.html, Component1.jsx, Component2.jsx}; see the UI kits section. Verify visually. Make one todo list item for each product/surface.
- If you were given a slide template, create sample slides in slides/{index.html, TitleSlide.jsx, ComparisonSlide.jsx, BigQuoteSlide.jsx, etc}. If no sample slides were given, don't create them. Create an HTML file per slide type; if decks were provided, copy their style. Use the visual foundations and bring in logos + other assets. Register each slide HTML via register_assets with viewport {width: 1280, height: 720} so the 16:9 frame scales to fit the card.
- Register each UI kit's index.html as its own card via register_assets with viewport {width: <kit's design width>, height: <above-fold height>} — the declared height caps what's shown, so pick the portion worth previewing.
- Update README.md with a short "index" pointing the reader to the other files available. This should serve as a manifest of the root folder, plus a list of ui kits, etc.
- Create SKILL.md file (details below)
- You are done! The Design System tab shows every registered card. Do NOT summarize your output; just mention CAVEATS (e.g. things you were unable to do or unsure) and have a CLEAR, BOLD ASK for the user to help you ITERATE to make things PERFECT.

UI kit details:
- UI Kits are high-fidelity visual + interaction recreations of interfaces. They cut corners on functionality -- they are not 'real production code' -- but they provide high-fidelity UI components. Your UI kits should be pixel-perfect recreations, created by reading the original UI code if possible, or using figma's get-design-context. They should be modular and reusable, so they can easily be pieced together for real designs. UI kits should recreate key screens in the product as click-thru prototypes. a UI kit's index.html must look like a typical view of the product. These are recreations, not storybooks.
- To start, update the todo list to contain these steps for each product: (1) Explore codebase + components in Figma (design context) and code, (2) Create 3-5 core screens for each product (e.g. homepage or app) with interactive click-thru components, (3) Iterate visually on the designs 1-2x, cross-referencing with design context.
- Figure out the core products from this company/codebase. There may be one, or a few. (e.g. mobile app, marketing website, docs website).
- Each UI kit must contain JSX components (well-factored; small, neat) for core UI elements (e.g. sidebars, composers, file panels, hero units, headers, footers, buttons, fields, menus, blog posts, video players, settings screens, login, etc).
- The index.html file should demonstrate an interactive version of the UI (e.g a chat app would show you a login screen, let you create a chat, send a message, etc, as fake)
- You should get the visuals exactly right, using design context or codebase import. Don't copy component implementations exactly; make simple mainly-cosmetic versions. It's important to copy.
- Focus on good component coverage, not replicating every single section in a design.
- Do not invent new designs for UI kits. The job of the UI kit is to replicate the existing design, not create a new one. Copy the design, don't reinvent it. If you do not see it in the project, omit, or leave purposely blank with a disclaimer.

Guidance
- Run independently without stopping unless there's a crucial blocker (E.g. lack of Figma access to a pasted link; lack of codebase access).
- When creating slides and UI kits, avoid cutting corners on iconography; instead, copy icon assets in! Do not create halfway representations of iconography using hand-rolled SVG, emoji, etc.
- CRITICAL: Do not recreate UIs from screenshots alone unless you have no other choice! Use the codebase, or Figma's get-design-context, as a source of truth. Screenshots are much lossier than code; use screenshots as a high-level guide but always find components in the codebase if you can!
- Avoid these visual motifs unless you are sure you see them in the codebase or Figma: bluish-purple gradients, emoji cards, cards with rounded corners and colored left-border only
- Avoid reading SVGs -- this is a waste of context! If you know their usage, just copy them and then reference them.
- When using Figma, use get-design-context to understand the design system and components being used. Screenshots are ONLY useful for high-level guidance. Make sure to expand variables and child components to get their content, too. (get_variable_defs)
- Create these files in the ROOT of the project unless asked not to. For example, README.md should be at the root, not in a folder!
- Stop if key resources are unnecessible: ff a codebase was attached or mentioned, but you are unable to access it via local_ls, etc, you MUST stop and ask the user to re-attach it using the Import menu. These get reattached often; do not complete a design system if you get a disconnect! Similarly, if a Figma url is inaccessible, stop and ask the user to rectify. NEVER go ahead spending tons of time making a design system if you cannot access all the resources the user gave you.

SKILL.md
- When you are done, we should make this file cross-compatible with Agent SKills in case the user wants to download it and use it in Claude Code.
- Create a SKILL.md file like this:

<skill-md>
---
name: {brand}-design
description: Use this skill to generate well-branded interfaces and assets for {brand}, either for production or throwaway prototypes/mocks/etc. Contains essential design guidelines, colors, type, fonts, assets, and UI kit components for protoyping.
user-invocable: true
---

Read the README.md file within this skill, and explore the other available files.
If creating visual artifacts (slides, mocks, throwaway prototypes, etc), copy assets out and create static HTML files for the user to view. If working on production code, you can copy assets and read the rules here to become an expert in designing with this brand.
If the user invokes this skill without any other guidance, ask them what they want to build or design, ask some questions, and act as an expert designer who outputs HTML artifacts _or_ production code, depending on the need.
</skill-md>

content.content:
Now I'll build the system. Cormorant Garamond and Inter are both on Google Fonts so no substitution needed.Now let me build the preview cards. I'll create a shared base and batch out cards.The Tan Lerida design system is built. A few things to flag and a clear ask to iterate on.

**Caveats**
- **Mood images** (`mood-portrait-warm.jpg`, `mood-collection-red.jpg`) carry placeholder branding for *other* fictional fashion houses (Arowwai, Borcelle). I used them as composition/light moodboards only — they cannot ship as Tan Lerida marketing.
- **Emblem master** is a photographed PNG only. A vector SVG would let it work as a favicon, footer pin, and small-size brand mark without artifacting.
- **Icon set** — Lucide via CDN is a flagged substitution. Swap it for a proprietary set if/when one exists.
- **Fonts** — Cormorant Garamond + Inter are loaded from Google Fonts. Self-hosted `.woff2` would be better for production.
- **No codebase or Figma** was provided, so the web UI kit is a *first canonical surface*, not a recreation. The voice, type, color, layout rules all come straight from the brief.
- **Australian launch specifics** (price points, retail partner, exact launch date, postal/legal copy) are not yet in the brief — placeholders used.

**The bold ask**

To take this from good to perfect, I need three things from you:

1. **Real first-party photography** — a small set of product shots (4:5 hero crop) and one editorial portrait shot in the mood the brief describes (warm, real grain, single key light). I'll swap the placeholders and re-publish the kit.
2. **A vector emblem** + the **gold wordmark in SVG** if you have it. The PNG works at hero size; vectors unlock everything else.
3. **The first three real product names, prices in AUD, materials, and dimensions** — even one finished product page lets me pressure-test the type and PDP layout against actual copy instead of my proxies (The Carry / Hold / Day).

Drop those into the chat and I'll iterate. While you collect them, tell me which **single screen** you'd like me to push next: a marketing landing for the Sydney launch, the materials/tannery long-form page, or a checkout flow.

---
brand_name: Tan Lerida
description: A complete leather house from the family that makes the leather.
last_updated: 2026-05-02
---

# Tan Lerida

## Logo
/mnt/user-data/uploads/Final_Logo.png
Script wordmark in signature gold on deep black. Do not stretch. Do not place on busy backgrounds. Minimum clear space = full logo height on all sides.

## Colors
- **Primary:** `#0A0A0A` — Ink Black. Default background for hero sections, packaging, and dark-mode layouts.
- **Secondary:** `#C9A84C` — Signature Gold. Logo color. Use for wordmark, key typographic accents, and select UI highlights.
- **Accent:** `#2C4A3E` — Tannery Green. Use for CTAs, buttons, and key callouts only. Never in large areas.
- **Neutrals:**
  - Text (on light): `#0A0A0A`
  - Text (on dark): `#F8F8F6`
  - Muted: `#8A8A8A`
  - Background (light): `#F8F8F6`
  - Background (dark): `#0A0A0A`

## Typography
- **Display + headings:** Cormorant Garamond — elegant, craft-weighted serif. Use for product names, hero headlines, pull quotes.
- **Body:** Inter — clean, neutral, highly legible at all sizes. Use for descriptions, UI copy, product details.
- **Code/data callouts:** Inter Mono (optional, for specs, measurements, certifications)

## Voice & Tone
Assured. Rooted. Understated. Craft-obsessed. Unapologetic. Warm.

- **Assured** — speaks without hedging, never over-explains or qualifies
- **Rooted** — India is not a footnote, it is the foundation; provenance is stated as fact
- **Understated** — lets product and process do the talking; no overclaiming
- **Craft-obsessed** — details and process are always specific, never vague
- **Unapologetic** — does not position relative to Western luxury; stands on its own terms
- **Warm** — human, not cold; the warmth of a maker, not a corporation

## Brands Admired
- **Nicobar** — Indian identity worn with ease; warm editorial aesthetic; craft and culture as natural context, never performed
- **Nappa Dori** — handcrafted leather done with restraint; product-forward; unpretentious premium
- **Cuyana** — "fewer, better things" philosophy; strong white space; minimal copy that trusts the product
- **Reformation** — confident brand voice; sustainability woven into identity without being preachy; treats customer as an equal
- **Bottega Veneta** — quality so evident the logo becomes unnecessary; architectural restraint; no visible branding needed

## Avoid

### Visual
- Fast fashion lookbook aesthetic — models in motion, hair blowing, white background lifestyle shots
- Loud hardware — oversized logos, gold chains, exterior branding beyond the most restrained mark
- Startup colorways — millennial pink, sage green, dusty blue, pastel anything
- Overly processed photography — heavy retouching, fake textures, CGI leather
- Cliché craft photography — hands touching leather in golden hour, artisan looking thoughtfully at work, product floating on white, flat lays with flowers
- Heavy black + gold as the dominant combination (cliché luxury, the Sabyasachi problem)
- Didot or Bodoni clones — too Dior, too borrowed
- Geometric sans-serifs like Futura — too Swiss, no warmth
- Display scripts — too festive, too wedding
- Canva template typography

### Copy
- Heritage bragging — "70 years of excellence" stated flatly as a credential
- Sustainability press release language — "committed to sustainable practices"
- Positioning against the West — "rivals European luxury" or "Made in India, global quality"
- Luxury checklist words — premium, exclusive, bespoke, opulent, sophisticated, curated, elevated
- Lifestyle overclaiming — vague atmospheric copy with no material, process, or place anchor
- Humble startup voice — "We believe that..." / "Our journey began..." / "We're on a mission to..."
- Forced Indianness — Hindi words or Sanskrit references dropped in to perform heritage

### Colors to never use
- Millennial Pink — trend-led, dated
- Sage / Eucalyptus Green — owned by wellness
- Off-white / Cream as primary — too D2C "clean"
- Bright or electric tones — wrong register
- Pastel anything — signals seasonal, not timeless

## Target Audience

### Primary — "The Earned Life"
Age 32-48. Mumbai, Delhi, Bangalore, Hyderabad — or relocated to London, Dubai, Singapore, New York, still considers India home. Built their wealth through competence: founders, consultants, architects, surgeons, creative directors. Money is present but not the point. Buys things once, gets it right. Researches before purchasing, reads the materials page before the product page. Deeply allergic to being sold to. Has retired the logo phase. Wants one bag that goes everywhere and gets better every year. Is not replacing their current leather bag because it wore out — they are replacing it because they are ready for something that reflects who they are now.

### Secondary — "The Global Indian"
NRI or PIO, 28-45. Has perspective on India from living outside it. Seeks genuinely Indian craft — not tourist-Indian, not export-Indian. Slightly evangelical about good Indian brands when they find them. The moment of saying "it's an Indian leather house, they make their own leather" is partly why they buy.

### Tertiary — "The Gifter"
Age 38-58. Sends corporate gifts that reflect their own taste. Has stopped giving pen sets. Needs the story to be easy to tell because the story is part of the gift. Also: the parent buying a child's first real leather good, the spouse replacing a worn wallet, the friend who wants to give something remembered.

### Who is NOT the customer
- The person who wants visible logos
- The trend-follower
- The person who needs to be convinced Indian craft is world-class
- The bargain hunter — this is not a value conversation, it is a lasting conversation

## Usage Notes (for downstream skills)
- Accent color (`#2C4A3E`) is for CTAs and key highlights only — never large background areas.
- Gold (`#C9A84C`) is a brand color, not a decorative one — use with restraint.
- Lead with **Assured** and **Craft-obsessed** in short copy; soften to **Warm** and **Rooted** in long-form.
- Photography direction: product should look like it has already lived a life, or is about to. Real grain. Real light. No retouching of texture.
- Copy must always be anchored to something specific — a material, a process, a place. Never atmospheric without substance.
- The single test for every visual and verbal decision: does this look like a brand that has something to prove, or a brand that has something to share? Always the latter.

input.content:
# Tan Lerida — Design System

> A complete leather house from the family that makes the leather.
> Indian by origin. Built for the earned life. Launching in Australia.

Tan Lerida is a premium-mass leather brand: bags, wallets, belts, small
goods. Vertically integrated — the family runs the tannery — so provenance
is stated as **fact, not story**. The system that follows expresses that
posture: assured, rooted, understated, craft-obsessed, unapologetic, warm.

---

## What's in this folder

| File / folder | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `README.md` | This file — brand context, content, visual & icon foundations. |
| `colors_and_type.css` | All design tokens — colors, type, spacing, radii, motion. Import everywhere. |
| `assets/` | Logos (wordmark + emblem), mood imagery, sample product imagery placeholders. |
| `fonts/` | Webfont references — both type families are loaded via Google Fonts, see note. |
| `preview/` | Self-contained design-system reference cards. |
| `ui_kits/web/` | Marketing & e-commerce web UI kit — homepage, PDP, journal article, etc. |
| `SKILL.md` | Agent-skill manifest. |

## Sources we worked from

The user provided these files in `uploads/` (originals retained there):

- `uploads/brand (1).md` — full brand brief (positioning, audience, voice, what to avoid).
- `uploads/Final Logo (2).png` — **gold script wordmark** "Tan Lerida".
- `uploads/TL Emblem (1).png` — **TL emblem in white** photographed on black leather.
- `uploads/Untitled design.jpg`, `(1).jpg`, `(2).jpg` — three mood references.
  Note: the second and third reference images carry placeholder branding for other
  fictional fashion houses ("Arowwai", "Borcelle"). They were treated as
  **mood-only** — composition, photography mood, density, type rhythm — not as
  literal Tan Lerida assets.

No codebase or Figma was attached; this system was built from the brief and the
visual references. Flagged substitutions are noted at the end.

---

## Content Fundamentals

The Tan Lerida voice is six things at once: **assured, rooted, understated,
craft-obsessed, unapologetic, warm**. In short copy lead with assured and
craft-obsessed; in long-form, soften toward warm and rooted.

### Posture
- **Statements, not pitches.** Periods, not exclamations. No hedging
  ("we believe", "we're on a mission"). No qualifying ("genuinely premium",
  "truly handcrafted"). The thing is the thing.
- **Provenance is fact.** "Tanned in our family's tannery in Tamil Nadu since
  1972." Not "heritage of 50+ years."
- **Specifics over atmospherics.** Always anchor to a material, a process, or
  a place. "Vegetable-tanned in chestnut bark for 40 days" beats "lovingly
  crafted with traditional methods" every time.
- **No positioning against the West.** No "rivals European luxury", no
  "Made in India, global quality". Indian craft does not need that scaffolding.

### Casing
- **Sentence case for everything except short brand modules.** Headlines,
  buttons, navigation, page titles — all sentence case.
- **All-caps reserved** for eyebrows, micro-labels, and the rare brand
  module ("THE TANNERY", "FROM CHENNAI"), with **0.18em tracking** so it
  reads as a label, not a shout.

### Voice — first / second person
- **"You" exists** but is rare and direct. Used in product copy and care
  instructions. Never wheedling.
- **"We" is operational, not confessional.** "We tan the leather we use" is
  fine. "We're on a journey to…" is not.
- **The customer is an equal.** Never explained to. Never sold to. Trust
  that they read the materials page before the product page.

### Numbers, units, dates
- Days of tanning, grams, mm, years — written out as numerals with units.
  `40 days · 1.4 mm temple · est. 1972`.
- Prices in AUD for Australia. No "from $" — name the price.

### Emoji & special characters
- **No emoji.** Anywhere. Including in long-form, social, support replies.
- **Unicode as punctuation only.** En-dash for ranges (`Mon–Sat`), middle dot
  for facts (`Veg-tanned · Tamil Nadu · 40 days`), proper curly quotes.
- **No icons used as decoration.** When an icon appears, it carries weight
  (filter, close, cart). See ICONOGRAPHY.

### Examples (do / don't)

| ✗ Avoid | ✓ Tan Lerida |
|---|---|
| "Lovingly crafted with 70 years of heritage." | "Tanned by my grandfather. Tanned by my father. Tanned by us." |
| "Premium, exclusive, opulent leather goods curated for the modern connoisseur." | "Bags, wallets, belts. Vegetable-tanned. Made to outlast the way you carry them." |
| "Our journey began with a dream…" | "The tannery has been in the family since 1972. We started making bags in 2019." |
| "Ethically sourced & sustainably crafted ✨" | "Vegetable-tanned in chestnut bark over 40 days. The waste is composted on site." |
| "Discover our exclusive collection!" | "The Carry. The Hold. The Day. Three things, made well." |
| "Made in India, global quality." | "Made in Chennai." |

### Australian launch — copy notes
- Spell with **Australian conventions**: *colour, organise, tannery (same)*.
- Reference local context **only when it earns its place** ("Stocked at
  Pitt Street from June 4."). Never "designed for Australia" — the leather is
  the same leather wherever it ships.
- Provenance is still India. The Australian moment is the *invitation*, not
  the origin story.

---

## Visual Foundations

The look is **architectural restraint**. Lots of air. Few elements per view.
Type does most of the work. Imagery does the rest. Color is used like
spice — gold is a brand accent, never decoration; green is a CTA color,
never a background. The combination of black + gold *dominantly* is a
deliberate avoid (too cliché-luxury / Sabyasachi). Bone-paper-warmth is
the everyday register; black is the dramatic register.

### Color system

Three families, used at very different volumes:

- **Surfaces** (most of the page): `--tl-bone #F8F8F6`, `--tl-bone-2 #EFEDE8`,
  `--tl-paper #E7E3DA`, and the dark register `--tl-ink #0A0A0A`.
  Pages live primarily on `--tl-bone`. Hero / editorial moments may flip to
  `--tl-ink`. Section breaks step down to `--tl-bone-2` or `--tl-paper`,
  never to a new hue.
- **Brand & accent** (under 5% of any view): `--tl-gold #C9A84C` for the
  wordmark and rare typographic accents; `--tl-tannery #2C4A3E` for CTAs,
  focus rings, and the occasional key callout. Tannery green never covers
  large areas.
- **Imagery tones**: warm cognac, rust, deep brown — these live *in
  photographs*, not as UI fills.

The avoid list (millennial pink, sage, dusty blue, off-white as primary
brand statement, electric tones) is permanent. So is "no large
black-and-gold blocks". When black and gold sit together, gold is letterforms
on black — never a gold panel on black.

### Typography

- **Cormorant Garamond** — every heading, every pull quote, every product
  name. Weight 400 with selective italic for emphasis. Letter-spacing
  tightens at display sizes (`-0.012em` at 84px). Never weight 700+ for
  headings — keep the air in the strokes.
- **Inter** — every paragraph, button, label, navigation element. Weight 400
  default; 500 for buttons and eyebrows; 300 for leads and editorial
  intros (very large, thin, generous line-height).
- **JetBrains Mono** — measurements, certifications, SKUs. Used like a
  caption tag, never for paragraphs.
- **Eyebrow style**: 12px, weight 500, all-caps, **0.18em tracking**, muted
  color. Sits 24–32px above an H1.

### Spacing & layout

- Base unit **4px**, scale: 4, 8, 12, 16, 24, 32, 48, 64, 96, 128.
- Section padding generous: vertical rhythm of `--sp-9` (96px) between
  sections on web; `--sp-7` (48px) within sections.
- Two grids: a 12-col 1240px **product** grid, and an 8-col 880px **editorial**
  grid for journal/long-form. Single-column is allowed — and preferred — for
  manifesto pages.
- Asymmetry is encouraged. A headline pinned to col 1, product image at
  col 7–12. Don't center everything.

### Backgrounds, imagery, texture

- **No gradients on UI.** A gradient may exist *as a vignette inside an image*
  (natural light), never as a background fill or button.
- **No repeating patterns.** No texture noise overlays except the very subtle
  "paper" of `--tl-paper` if a section needs to break. Real grain comes from
  real photos.
- **Photography**: warm, unretouched-feeling, real light. Outdoor afternoon
  light or studio with a single warm key. The leather should look like it
  has lived a life or is about to. Never floating-on-white catalog shots.
  Models when present are at ease — wearing, not posing.
- **Full-bleed imagery** is the default for hero and editorial moments.
  Cropped close — half-body, half-product — over wide establishing shots.

### Animation

Slow and certain. Easing of `cubic-bezier(0.2, 0.7, 0.2, 1)` (out) and
`cubic-bezier(0.3, 0, 0.2, 1)` (standard). Durations of **140ms / 240ms / 420ms**.

- **Fades**: 240ms ease-out, no upward drift on most things.
- **Hover on product imagery**: scale 1 → 1.02 over 420ms. Nothing more.
- **Page transitions** (if any): a single 420ms fade. No wipes, no slides.
- **No bounces.** No springs. No "delight" micro-interactions.
- Reduced-motion: every transition collapses to 0ms.

### Hover & press states

- **Buttons (primary, tannery)**: hover darkens to `--tl-tannery-2`. No
  scale, no shadow change.
- **Buttons (ghost / link)**: hover underlines. The underline animates from
  left in 240ms, never appears all at once.
- **Cards (product tiles)**: hover lifts the image scale to 1.02; the
  caption text shifts color from `--fg-2` to `--fg`. No shadow change.
- **Press**: `transform: translateY(0.5px)` plus `inset 0 1px 2px rgba(0,0,0,0.10)`
  so the surface feels pressed, not lifted.
- **Focus**: 2px ring of `--tl-tannery` at 2px offset. Not gold — gold is for
  brand, not for system feedback.

### Borders, dividers, radii

- **Hairlines, not boxes.** A divider is `1px` of `--tl-line` on light or
  `--tl-line-dark` on dark. Cards rarely have full borders; they sit on
  background tone change instead.
- **Corner radii**: `0`, `2px` (inputs / chips), `4px` (small surfaces),
  `8px` (cards), `14px` (image frames). The brand never uses pill buttons
  for primary CTAs — primary buttons are 2px radius, almost rectangular.
- **Pill shape** is reserved for tags and filters.

### Shadow / elevation

Used sparingly. The system has four elevations and most pages use only
the first two:

- `--shadow-0` — hairline ring (replaces a border on cards).
- `--shadow-1` — barely-there lift, for floating cart bar and toasts.
- `--shadow-2` — modal/menu surface.
- `--shadow-3` — full-screen sheet on dark.

No inner glows. No colored shadows. No long blurs.

### Transparency, blur, gradients

- **Glassmorphism is forbidden.** No backdrop-filter on UI surfaces.
- A **protection gradient** (black, 0 → 0.5 alpha, 200px tall) may appear at
  the bottom of full-bleed hero imagery to seat caption text. Never as a
  decorative overlay.
- A capsule (solid filled chip) is preferred over a transparent one when
  text sits over an image.

### Cards

A "card" in Tan Lerida is **a tonal block, not a boxed thing**.

- Product card: image (4:5), 24px gap, then product name (serif H4), price
  (sans 14), one descriptor line (`tl-caption`). No border. No shadow.
  Background is the page.
- Editorial / journal card: image (3:2 or 16:9), 16px gap, eyebrow (KICKER),
  serif H3 title, 1-line muted dek. Card extends edge-to-edge of its grid
  cell with no rounding above 8px.
- Surface card (cart line, settings): bone-2 surface, 8px radius,
  `--shadow-0` ring, 24px inner padding.

### Layout rules / fixed elements

- **Header**: 64px tall, transparent over hero, becomes solid bone with
  hairline divider on scroll past 80px. Never sticky on long-form articles.
- **Footer**: ink-black; serif TL emblem at top-left, link columns in sans,
  newsletter CTA in tannery green, fine print in mute.
- **Mini cart**: right sheet, 420px wide, ink-black surface, bone text.
- **No floating "chat with us" bubbles.** Customer service lives in the
  footer.

### A single test, repeated

> *Does this look like a brand that has something to prove, or a brand that
> has something to share?*
> Always the latter.

---

## Iconography

Tan Lerida iconography is **utility-first, never decorative**. Icons appear
when they replace a label that would otherwise be redundant (search, cart,
close, account, menu) — and nowhere else. Hero sections, value propositions,
features, and benefits are written, not iconed. There is no "three icons in a
row with a heading under each" pattern.

### What is allowed

- A small set of **outline icons**, 1.5px stroke, 24px box, square caps,
  rounded joins. Color always matches the text color it's adjacent to —
  icons are never given a brand fill of their own.
- The **TL emblem** (see `assets/emblem-on-leather.png`, also extracted as
  `assets/emblem-mark.svg` placeholder) appears as a brand pin in the
  footer, on packaging, and on the splash of the app — never inline within
  body copy.
- The **wordmark** is the navigation logotype.

### What is not used

- **No emoji**, ever. Not in copy, not in social, not in support replies.
- **No filled / "Material" icons.** The fill weight is wrong for the brand.
- **No iconography of leather, hands, hammers, cows, scissors, or other
  craft clichés.** When craft is shown, it is photographed, not pictogrammed.
- **No country flags as icons** — country is set in the footer language
  switcher as text ("Australia · AUD").
- **No social icons inflated into hero modules.** Social presence sits in
  the footer at 16px, single line.

### Implementation

The icon set is **Lucide** (1.5px outline, square geometry — closest match
to the brand's intent). It is loaded from CDN in the UI kit:

```html
<script src="https://unpkg.com/lucide@latest/dist/umd/lucide.min.js"></script>
```

A curated subset is used: `search, x, menu, user, shopping-bag, heart,
chevron-right, chevron-down, plus, minus, instagram, arrow-up-right`.
**This is a flagged substitution** — there is no proprietary Tan Lerida
icon set in the brief. Lucide was chosen because its proportions align
(thin stroke, generous internal counters). If the team has a custom set,
swap by replacing the CDN reference in `ui_kits/web/index.html`.

The TL emblem is provided in two forms:
- `assets/emblem-on-leather.png` — the original photographed asset, for
  rich brand moments.
- `assets/emblem-mark.svg` — a vector reproduction of the emblem outline
  for inline UI use (footer, favicon, app splash).

---

## Flagged substitutions & open questions

- **Fonts**: Cormorant Garamond and Inter are both loaded via Google Fonts.
  No custom font files were provided, and none are needed for these
  families. If self-hosting is preferred for the production launch, place
  `.woff2` files in `fonts/` and switch the `@import` in
  `colors_and_type.css` to a local `@font-face`.
- **Icon set**: Lucide via CDN, see above. Confirm or replace with a
  proprietary set.
- **Emblem SVG**: `assets/emblem-mark.svg` is a simplified vector, not the
  master file. Please supply the original vector if available.
- **Mood images** (`mood-portrait-warm.jpg`, `mood-collection-red.jpg`)
  carry placeholder branding for other fictional houses. They are useful
  as moodboards (composition, light, density) but **must not be used as
  Tan Lerida marketing assets**. Replace with first-party photography
  before any public use.
- **Australian launch specifics** — pricing, retail partner, launch date —
  are not yet in the brief. The system reserves space for them in the
  homepage hero and journal templates.

---

## Index

- [`colors_and_type.css`](colors_and_type.css) — tokens.
- [`assets/`](assets/) — logos, mood imagery, emblem.
- [`preview/`](preview/) — design-system reference cards.
- [`ui_kits/web/`](ui_kits/web/) — marketing + e-commerce kit.
- [`SKILL.md`](SKILL.md) — agent skill manifest.

# Tan Lerida — Web UI Kit

A high-fidelity recreation of the Tan Lerida e-commerce experience. This kit
demonstrates header → hero → pillars → product row → manifesto → tannery
spotlight → journal → footer, plus a working **mini cart** and a
**product detail page**.

## Files
- `index.html` — interactive demo. Click a product card → PDP. Add to bag → cart sheet.
- `kit.css` — tokens-driven shared styles. Imports `../../colors_and_type.css`.
- `Components.jsx` — `TLHeader`, `TLFooter`, `TLEyebrow`, `TLTag`, `TLProductCard`.
- `Sections.jsx` — `TLHero`, `TLPillars`, `TLProductRow`, `TLManifesto`, `TLTannery`, `TLJournalRow`.
- `CartAndPDP.jsx` — `TLCart` (right sheet on ink), `TLProductDetail`, `Detail` (accordion).

## Notes
- Imagery is mood-only — see `../../README.md` for replacement guidance.
- No real codebase or Figma was provided; UI was built directly from the
  brief's visual rules. Treat as the system's first canonical surface, not
  a recreation of an existing page.
- Click-throughs simulated: any of the three home product cards opens the
  same shared PDP shell, populated with that product's data.

contentBlocks.text:
The Tan Lerida design system is built. A few things to flag and a clear ask to iterate on.

**Caveats**
- **Mood images** (`mood-portrait-warm.jpg`, `mood-collection-red.jpg`) carry placeholder branding for *other* fictional fashion houses (Arowwai, Borcelle). I used them as composition/light moodboards only — they cannot ship as Tan Lerida marketing.
- **Emblem master** is a photographed PNG only. A vector SVG would let it work as a favicon, footer pin, and small-size brand mark without artifacting.
- **Icon set** — Lucide via CDN is a flagged substitution. Swap it for a proprietary set if/when one exists.
- **Fonts** — Cormorant Garamond + Inter are loaded from Google Fonts. Self-hosted `.woff2` would be better for production.
- **No codebase or Figma** was provided, so the web UI kit is a *first canonical surface*, not a recreation. The voice, type, color, layout rules all come straight from the brief.
- **Australian launch specifics** (price points, retail partner, exact launch date, postal/legal copy) are not yet in the brief — placeholders used.

**The bold ask**

To take this from good to perfect, I need three things from you:

1. **Real first-party photography** — a small set of product shots (4:5 hero crop) and one editorial portrait shot in the mood the brief describes (warm, real grain, single key light). I'll swap the placeholders and re-publish the kit.

A complete leather house

The hide. Tanned in our drum.

Forty days in chestnut bark. We stop turning the drum when the leather smells right — not when the timer ends.

40 days in chestnut bark, and what changes on day 38.
